description: This figure presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, comprising three subplots: (a) Nyquist plot showing the imaginary component (-Zim) versus real component (Zre) of impedance, where the capacitive arc diameter represents the polarization resistance; (b) Bode magnitude plot displaying the impedance modulus |Z| as a function of frequency; and (c) Bode phase plot showing the phase angle versus frequency. Four sample conditions are compared: S0 (as-hot-rolled without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C, 8.7 μm grain size), S1050 (solution-treated at 1050°C), and S1150 (solution-treated at 1150°C, 32 μm grain size). The EIS data reveal that S950 exhibits the largest capacitive arc in the Nyquist plot and highest impedance modulus, indicating superior corrosion resistance, while S0 shows the lowest resistance due to high dislocation density and κ-carbides. The Bode phase plot shows two time constants with maximum phase angles between 65° and 75° at intermediate frequencies, characteristic of non-ideal capacitive responses. An equivalent circuit model (inset in Fig. 8a) with two constant phase elements (Q1 and Q2) is employed for fitting, where Rs represents solution resistance, R1 and Q1 correspond to the diffusion resistance and CPE at the solution/film interface, and R2 and Q2 represent the oxide film resistance and CPE, respectively. The results demonstrate that fine grain structure (S950) promotes the formation of a dense, Al-rich (90.17 at%) oxide film with low defect density (ND 0.26×10²¹/cm³), providing optimal protection, whereas grain coarsening (S1050, S1150) reduces grain boundary density, leading to discontinuous oxide film growth with higher Fe/Mn content and degraded corrosion resistance.
